clean_story:
Numbers with hidden tricks. Truth stretched by math.

The phrase how statistics lie isn’t about false numbers—it’s about true numbers used to mislead, like a mirror that shows your face but hides the smudge on your cheek.

Statistics don’t lie alone; people twist them. A classic trick: cherry-picking ranges. Say ice cream sales rise 50% from January to July. True—but if you start in freezing December, the jump looks even bigger. The same data, framed differently, tells opposite stories. Another method: averages that deceive. If five people share ten cookies, the "average" is two per person—but one might have eight, another none. The mean hides the greed.

Graphs lie too. A line climbing steeply can look dramatic if the vertical axis starts at 98 instead of zero. Your brain sees a mountain; the real change is a molehill. Politicians and ads exploit this: "Crime dropped 10%!"—but if it was already rare, 10% might mean two fewer incidents.

Even percentages play games. "Our product is 90% effective!" sounds amazing—until you learn it only works for 90% of a very specific group, like left-handed plumbers. The fine print buries the truth.

Three related subjects are data cherry-picking tricks, how graphs manipulate eyes, and why humans trust numbers too much.
